segments.text:
Lecture 9: 
Building Phylogenetic Trees
Reasons to build a 
phylogenetic tree?
1)
Classify sequences
2)
Figure out when your protein appeared
3)
Predict functions of uncharacterized proteins by showing they are related to 
characterized proteins
- Orthologs or paralogs?
4)
Structure function analysis: 
- Proteins (or protein regions) with unusually long branches?  
Yeast
Human
Fly
Myosin V
Conventional Myosin
Myosin I
a-cardiac
b-cardiac
➥Change of function!

Vocabulary
• Clade
• Monophyletic
• Polyphyletic
• Outgroup
• Root
• Node
• Polytomy
Smaller clades can be nested within larger ones
Known outgroups are useful for rooting
phylogenetic trees
Which is closer to humans: 
Chimps or gorillas? 
Need an outgroup! Reasonable outgroup?
Clade:
a monophyletic group
• A single branch 
• A group that includes 
nothing outside that group
Are the following clades:
• Mammals
• Vertebrates
• Flying vertebrates
• Fish
Outgroup:
Branch basal to all others in the tree

How to build a phylogenetic tree
1) Identify, align all sequences
• Find sequences via BLAST or PSI-BLAST
• Align via standard software 
ClustalX?
More sophisticated: Clustal Omega, MUSCLE, PASTA….
• See if the alignment looks good. If not, use your judgement!
Options include:
- Remove poorly aligned sequences
- Ask machine to realign poorly aligned sequences
- Cut off N- or C- terminal extensions (confuse Clustal)
- Use a more sophisticated program
2) Determine phylogenetic relationships
• use PROTEIN SEQUENCE!!!
• use only conserved core of alignment
Trim final alignment using Jalview
• best to use more than one approach
3) Estimate confidence in observed groupings:  
Bootstrapping   10-1000 trials (choose based on size of sequence set!)
Other statistical approaches
➤tree is only as good as the alignment
How could you tell the 
difference between:
a)
A poorly aligned sequence?
b)
An unrelated sequence?
Approaches include:
“Neighbor Joining” (ClustalX)
More later….

What is bootstrapping?
Means of determining
Which part of tree can be trusted
Myosin V
Myosin II
Myosin I
Confidence Estimates:
High (> 95% of bootstraps)
Moderate (>80%)
Low (< 50%)
➥Bootstrapping
➤Problem:
In any tree
- Some branching patterns  
are firmly based on data
- Others are a result of noise
➤Solution:
- Take random samples of data
- Build trees from these samples
- See which groups are robust
NOTREDAME
NETREDUME
NUTRODOME
NNNEEAMME
NNNEEUMME
NNNOOOMME
OOTREDDEE
EETREDDEE
UUTRODDEE
Original alignment
Bootstrap sample 1
Bootstrap sample 2
Dot or number: indicates that the group external to the 
node was found in the indicated % of bootstrap trials.
Note: number implies nothing about the structure within 
the group, but simply that the group was observed

Building phylogenetic trees
➤Three basic methods
1. Distance
a)  Measure pairwise distance between sequences
b)  Use matrix of distances to build tree
- Group most closely related sequences, followed by successively more distant…
Neighbor Joining (ClustalX)
- Find tree that minimizes difference between observed distances in matrix
and distances measured along branches
various names Fitch-Margoliash
Note:  Distance appraoches collapse all differences between each pair of sequences into one number
2.  Parsimony
- Find tree that minimizes # evolutionary changes
PAUP
PHYLIP PROTPARS
3. Likelihood
- Find most likely tree (based on explicit model of evolution)
Bayesian (MrBayes)
Maximum likelihood (ML)
Note:  2 and 3 are SLOW!!!  Inexact with with large # sequences
4. Combined methods
- Use neighbor joining for first tree, then use ML (“FastTree”, PASTA)

List of useful phylogenetic analysis software programs
Most user-friendly: ClustalX – NJ algorithm
Tree-building
Pros:
• Easy to use 
• Easy bootstrapping
• FAST
• Works reasonably well for 
protein sequences
Cons:
• Uses an identity matrix (why bad?)
• NJ is frowned upon by the evolution 
community
• Bogs down if have huge numbers of 
sequences
More sophisticated NJ:
• PHYLIP package: “Protdist”
• MEGA package NJ 
Parsimony: PHYLIP ”Protpars”; PAUP
Multi-program packages:
• PHYLIP (classic)
• MEGA (the new thing…)
To find: Google…
Baysian, ML: MrBAYES, RaxML
Combined methods:  Good for large datasets!
• FASTTREE
• PASTA  alignment AND tree (!)

Assignment for Wednesday:
Make yourself familiar with using Archaeopteryx to draw trees
I suggest useing the two EB1 tree files (.phb) I uploaded to Piazza. 
• Make sure that you can use these trees to convince yourself that there are 
three EB1 proteins in verebrate organisms. 
• Make sure that you can zoom in and zoom out
• Be able to use the “search” tool to find sequences with specific names or 
parts of names
• Be able to move between and interpret various types of trees 
e.g, unrooted trees; different types of rooted trees
• Be able to reroot a tree at a desired sequence or node
• Play around with other options. 
For example, can you figure out how to color desired subsections of the tree?
